data.title: Skyward Specialty: Recent Developments Have Improved Its Investment Case
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article some months ago, Skyward Specialty Insurance Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:SKWD) doesn’t hold any specific competitive advantage in the P&C insurance sector and its valuation was not particularly attractive at the time for long-term investors. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed somewhat poorly since my last article, underperforming the market by some margin since then.

More recently, the company has reported its quarterly earnings related to Q2 2025, reporting a positive operating performance, and has reached an agreement to buy Apollo Group Holdings Limited for about $555 million.

Apollo is an insurer based in the U.S. that operates mainly in the Lloyd’s of London market, being focused on underwriting specialty insurance lines through syndicates. It makes a good business fit with Skyward considering that it expands Skyward’s presence in some specialty insurance lines and brings lines, especially related to new economy industries.

Apollo was established in 2010 and is partly owned by management, employees, and Alchemy, offering data-driven and creative solutions to several parts of the specialty insurance industry. Apollo has developed a relationship with Skyward over the past couple of years, collaborating several times, and Skyward believes the business combination provides a good cultural fit. Apollo’s management will therefore continue to run the business after the integration into the Skyward group, showing that the company sees this business as being well-run and wants to make a smooth transition into its group.

From a financial standpoint, Apollo has a very good growth history given that its gross written premiums have grown at a compounded annual growth rate of about 20% since its inception. Currently, Apollo manages some $1.5 billion of premiums in several specialty lines, generating significant fee-based earnings and has a capital-light business model. Skyward expects this acquisition to deliver double-digit adjusted operating EPS accretion in the first full year after the integration of Apollo, showing that it will have a significant impact on increasing Skyward’s position in the specialty market and also to support its evolution to a more capital-light model.

Regarding valuation, Skyward will pay less than 9x EBITDA expected for the full year 2025, being a reasonable price for a high-growth business that has a recurring business model. This acquisition will be made through a combination of cash and shares, with Apollo’s financial investors exiting from the company, while strategic investors and management will receive Skyward’s shares. Skyward has already reached a funding commitment from Barclays (BCS) to finance the cash component through debt, which will increase its financial leverage when the acquisition is completed. The deal is expected to close during the first quarter of 2026, assuming it gets all the required regulatory approvals.

This combination seems to make sense both financially and also from a business perspective, being an important step for Skyward to continue to diversify its operations by type of insurance risk and will also boost its digital and analytical capabilities. Indeed, its strategy has been to invest in technology and advanced analytics capabilities over the past few years to differentiate itself from competitors in the insurance industry, making this acquisition also interesting from this point.

Regarding underwriting profitability, Apollo’s combined ratio has been slightly below 95% over the past few years, which is a higher ratio than compared to Skyward, showing there is some room for the company to improve its underwriting profits ahead. This can be achieved by a combination of better underwriting capabilities by leveraging both companies’ platforms, but also by better claims management, namely by Apollo potentially using SkyBI, Skyward’s proprietary business intelligence platform.

Regarding its operating performance, Skyward reported its Q2 2025 earnings a couple of months ago, which maintained a positive operating momentum as the market backdrop has remained supportive for P&C companies.

However, not all insurance lines are performing well, and the company adjusted its risk appetite considering current market conditions. It pulled back from some casualty lines where loss inflation is a concern for profitability, allocating more capital toward other lines where it sees better returns in the future.

Given this backdrop, gross written premiums declined in global property and construction & energy solutions, while accident & health, agriculture, and specialty programs reported strong premium growth. Overall, its gross premiums increased by 17.3% YoY during the first half of 2025 to more than $1.1 billion, reflecting a supportive industry backdrop regarding both pricing and volumes. Its net premiums earned amounted to $595 million, or 61% of gross premiums written, being a stable ratio compared to the same period of last year.

Regarding its underwriting profitability, Skyward’s combined ratio was 90% in the first half of the year, being practically unchanged compared to H1 2024, impacted by some convective storms in the South and Midwest that resulted in higher claims costs than expected. Adjusted for catastrophic events, its combined ratio was 88.2% in H1 2025, improving by about 90 basis points (bps) compared to the same period of 2024. This shows that Skyward’s increased focus on underwriting profitability instead of chasing top-line growth is on the right path, being positive for underwriting performance in the near future.

Its investment income increased significantly to more than $10 million in H1 2025, up by 53% YoY, supported by higher interest rates as the company has deployed cash generation into its fixed income portfolio and has reinvested securities at higher yields, plus it also reported higher capital gains in the period compared to H1 2024.

Due to higher underwriting results and investment income, Skyward’s operating income increased to more than $100 million in the first half of 2025, up by 16% YoY, while its net income amounted to nearly $81 million (+19% YoY). Its annualized net return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability within the insurance sector, was 19% in the first half of 2025, being a very good level of profitability compared to peers.

For the full year, the company is well on track to reach its guidance of net income between $138-$150 million, and may even achieve a higher level if operating momentum continues.

Regarding its balance sheet, Skyward has a solid position given that its debt-to-capital ratio was 12% at the end of last June, being at a conservative level compared to other insurance companies and gives it flexibility to integrate Apollo during 2026 and increase its leverage position a little bit. Despite this strong financial profile, its strategy has been to reinvest cash flow generation into the company, namely in business growth and higher allocation into its investment portfolio, while historically has not distributed dividends. This strategy is not expected to change in the near term, especially considering that Skyward will have to integrate a sizeable acquisition over the coming quarters, but eventually it may decide to distribute dividends to shareholders in the future, as an important driver of insurance companies’ investment case is usually related to income.

Regarding its valuation, Skyward is currently trading slightly above 2x book value, a much cheaper valuation compared to when I last analyzed it, given that it was trading at 2.5x book value at the time. This derating is explained by a growing book value that was not reflected in a higher share price over the past few months. Considering that Skywards ROE is quite high, I see a valuation around 2.5x book value as being warranted, making Skyward somewhat undervalued right now. Compared to its peers, which include RLI Corp. (RLI), American Financial Group, Inc. (AFG), or ProAssurance Corporation (PRA) its valuation also seems to be compelling.

Conclusion

Skyward Specialty has reported a positive operating momentum in recent quarters and its recent agreement to acquire Apollo Group Holding Limited seems to make sense both financially and from a business perspective, while maintaining a sound financial profile at the same time. Given that its shares have lagged its peers and the overall stock market over the past few months, leading to a much lower valuation than it was trading some months ago, I now see Skyward as an interesting growth play in the insurance P&C market for long-term investors.

data.title: Home Sale Strength
data.description:
It was an eventful week for housing data as New Home Sales were released on Wednesday and Existing Home Sales came out yesterday. Working in reverse order, Existing Home Sales came in ahead of forecasts on a national level, although the sales pace has remained sluggish relative to pre-pandemic or early post-pandemic levels. As shown below, on a regional basis, sales are strongest in the South by a solid margin, although that is historically normal. Current levels are hovering around the lows from the spring of 2020, and the same applies to the West and Northeast. As for the Midwest, sales are well below those lows.

Turning over to prices, trends have a bit more variation. Prices are highest in the West and that has been the case for over a decade now. With that said, the past few years since higher rates came into play have seen far less steady gains compared to the exponential move higher from 2020 to 2022. Likewise, the South has seen prices flatline in the past few years amid higher inventories. The Midwest and Northeast are entirely different stories. The torrid price gains of the early pandemic years have continued for those two regions, with only a minor dip occurring in late 2022/early 2023.

Turning over to New Home Sales data updated on Wednesday, there was a very strong print on a national scale, surging 20% MoM versus estimates of a small decline. The huge beat was driven by strong sales in completed homes, although sale of homes under construction also surged. Geographically, that strength was broad, with all regions ticking up during the month of August. While accounting for the smallest share of total homes, sales in the Northeast were up an impressive 72% MoM and sales in the South (the largest share of national sales and inventories) jumped by 24.7%. As shown in the second chart below, new homes for sale (inventories) have fallen meaningfully after the latest surge in sales in the South. Meanwhile, the Midwest now has some of the highest inventories for new homes since February 2009.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla Board Prints Billions In Pay As Musk Buys $1B, And I'm Shorting The Signal
data.description:
Why I am giving Strong Sell on TSLA

It seems to me that fewer and fewer people realize that Tesla (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:TSLA) is still primarily a car manufacturer. Sales in Q2 2025 fell by 12% YoY, and CEO Elon Musk himself spoke of a few tough quarters ahead. Two months have passed and something has changed, so suddenly the next few quarters are looking bright? I don't think so. The company is still not in a position to justify its premium valuation multiples.

Moreover, the overall macroeconomic situation is also working against these absurdly overvalued companies. Unfortunately, Tesla is not alone in this. The S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (QQQ) are at new all-time highs, while unemployment is climbing to 4.3% and inflation is accelerating to 2.9%. In the late economic cycle, this combination often precedes sharp selloffs, and the first to go are precisely these hyper-valued names like Tesla.

I would also like to mention my opinion on Elon Musk's recent purchase of shares. Many people are now talking about this purchase and saying that buying Tesla is now a "sure thing." On September 12, 2025, Musk bought approximately 2.5 million shares for $1 billion. At first glance, this seems impressive, but in essence, it means nothing when compared to the new mega bonus package that the board of directors is preparing for Musk. These new bonuses are expected to amount to $1 trillion over 10 years. Musk is not in a position where he needs this money, so what else should he do with it other than put it into the company? If I simply put two and two together, the billion-dollar purchase seems more like PR than any fundamental signal. The fundamental effect is negligible, and I would not hesitate to call it helicopter money.

The current bulls like to lean on FSD/robotaxis/AI. But these are all speculations about the future, not today's starting point based on hard data. If we look at the company's current reality, cash flow is dismal, margins are eroding due to discounts and price wars. The valuation is many times above the sector average. From my perspective, the current downside is greater than the upside. Tesla is closer to the automotive industry than to AI.

If anyone is still unfamiliar with Tesla

Tesla mainly manufactures cars. Its financial statements show two segments, Automotive and Energy. Most of the company's revenue still comes from Automotive (car sales + related services). The Energy segment (Megapack batteries, etc.) is only a small part of the business, which is growing but still accounts for a minority of revenue.

In Q2 2025, automotive sales fell by 16% (new car deliveries down 13% to 14%). If we take into account the company's overall 12% decline in sales, we have validation that automotive is the core business, and that is where pressure on margins is now occurring. Robotaxis and other fun stuff are more like PR for the future at this point.

Although the company talked about a turning point in Q2, when it will switch to AI/robotaxis, I am just taking note of this for now and not working with it further. It is not visible in the P&L, and the reality is that AI will not overtake revenues from automotive for the time being.

Why is this environment dangerous for Tesla

If Tesla only had weak numbers, let's say. However, I see a problem here in that even overly positive market sentiment cannot save the company's crazy valuations. The overall macro environment is currently set against these overvalued companies. The market is up, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq hitting new highs and breaking records. And here's the rub, because bad news and overvalued companies not at ATH are punished twice as fast as in a healthy macro environment.

The labor market is weakening, with only 22,000 new jobs added in August, while unemployment has already reached 4.3%. If we look at this data from an economic perspective, this is typical for the final phase of economic cycles. Inflation has accelerated again to +2.9% (core +3.1%). The Fed recently cut rates, but sticky inflation and a weaker labor market are a very ugly combination that has historically preceded major downturns. If we think about it comprehensively, from a broader perspective, we really don't have a favorable environment for risky assets, which I simply have to include Tesla in with its valuation.

For Tesla, this means that as soon as the current very fragile risk-on sentiment breaks, there will be harsh penalties. The company is currently valued as an AI visionary, while its cash flow comes mainly from Automotive, where sales are falling and margins are increasingly eroding. The narrative is a nice thing, but when it clashes with the numbers in the current environment, the numbers clearly win.

What happened below the line, and why is it a problem for the current valuation

I don’t like to repeat myself, but I would like to mention again that sales have fallen and margins are being pushed down. I would like to elaborate on this fact in the following few lines. Tesla produced approximately 410,000 vehicles in Q2, but only delivered 384,000. In other words, it created inventory, inventory is growing, and valuable capital is tied up. At the same time, the company changed its Model 3 prices in China by 3.7%, so pressure on ASP continues. We are in an environment where demand is weaker not only in Europe but also in China, so I don't see much chance of a return to more attractive margins. We can also find regulatory credits in the financial statements. We know from the past that these credits often masked part of the lost automotive margin. As margins continue to decline, the real economics of the vehicle business will become increasingly visible in the P&L. Tesla itself has stated that the decline in credits is one of the reasons for the decline in its automotive business.

The fact that the company is investing in AI also means higher R&D, which climbed 48% year-on-year and took a full 7% of sales. The company itself has confirmed that these are investments in AI and other programs. However, we know that profits come mainly from Automotive, so today's investments in FSD/robotaxis are taking profits from already weak results. A company that is supposed to justify such a valuation should have the opposite dynamics (revenue growth > cost growth).

The Energy segment is perhaps the only bright spot I can find in the company, but unfortunately, the numbers are still grim. Energy rose by 30.3%, mainly thanks to cheaper Megapack/Powerwall and the deployment of 9.6GWh. This is certainly great news for all shareholders, but in terms of volume, it is still not enough to offset the losses from the Automotive segment. Tesla has lowered its CaAPex outlook for 2025, but it is still weak. The shift from $10 billion to $9 billion is simply not enough. In the long term, it has also talked about $11 billion in the coming years. My interpretation of this information is that Tesla is trying to tighten the purse strings, but the capital intensity of the business remains high. Especially if it is to deliver autonomy and scale-up in battery systems.

Finally, I would like to mention that the key market (California) has seen a decline for seven quarters in a row. This is a very sensitive issue when it comes to home turf. Weaker demand at home usually precedes a broader trend in other areas. For a stock that is valued as an AI leader, this is a really bad financial situation unless demand and ASP improve quickly.

Comparison with cars and AI leaders, or why TSLA doesn't work for me

Let's look at both sectors simultaneously, the traditional automotive industry, which corresponds to the company's current revenue profile, and the true AI leaders.

If I compare Tesla to an automotive manufacturer, we have a forward P/E ratio of 160x-170x and an EV/EBITDA ratio of approximately 110x, which are multiples that no other large automotive company achieves. And I must point out that Tesla's revenues and margins are declining. For example, Toyota (NYSE: TM) has a forward P/E ratio of 7x-8x and an EV/Sales ratio of less than 1x. Toyota is performing well, and investors are paying significantly less for it. Or let's look at Ford (NYSE: F), where EV/EBITDA is 16x (which is a bit higher, but that's currently due to higher EV profitability), but even so, it's a ridiculous valuation compared to Tesla.

If I value Tesla as a car manufacturer, it seems extremely overvalued compared to companies like Toyota and Ford. And this is at a time when companies are seeing declining sales and ASP. This is simply unsustainable unless the company's fundamentals change quickly. Moreover, if we look at this segment as a whole, everyone is dealing with weaker demand for EVs, forecasts are being cut dramatically, and plans are being reevaluated.

For those who argue that Tesla should not be viewed as a traditional car manufacturer but as an AI company, fine. Let's compare it with the real titans of this sector. Nvidia (NVDA) currently has a forward P/E ratio of 31x-39x. Revenue growth and margins are excellent here, and FCF is also very strong. Then there is Microsoft (MSFT), which has a forward P/E ratio of approximately 33x and an EV/Sales ratio of 13x-14x. We are talking about stable double-digit growth, which Microsoft is delivering. In addition, it maintains gross margins of around 69%, and the company has massive FCF. Let's take a look at Apple (AAPL), about which I recently wrote an article saying that it is also overvalued, and yet compared to Tesla, it has a ridiculous valuation. Apple has a FWD P/E of around 32x, EV/EBITDA of 25x, and EV/Sales somewhere between 8x and 9x. It is also a premium company with a high moat, but even so, its valuation multiples are a third of those of Tesla.

So Tesla is overpriced even compared to the most expensive AI leaders. Its competitors have higher margins and better FCF, yet they trade at fractions of FWD P/E. If Tesla were to be valued at current multiples, it would have to deliver corresponding growth, which I do not see at this time.

Whether I look at Tesla through the lens of an AI player or an automaker (where it belongs), the probability of a price decline is higher than that of further growth. Tesla charges a luxury AI premium, but so far it can only deliver automotive results. Such a combination has no chance of lasting, especially in an environment of market peaks, a weakening labor market, and sticky inflation.

What could disprove my thesis, and what could prove it

If FSD/Robotaxi revenues were to be reported, the current valuations could start to make more sense. If Tesla launches paid rides in at least a few cities, obtains the necessary regulatory approvals, and demonstrates tangible APRU (stock $ per car per month), then it may be able to maintain its AI premium even without a rapid recovery in automotive margins.

Furthermore, Tesla could accelerate sales on Megapack. If NACS (network revenue) were also monetized, FCF could stabilize and offset weaker margins from Automotive. Indices are at highs, unemployment is rising, and inflation remains at higher levels. However, this does not mean that risk-on will break. The market can be irrational for a very long time, and we may run out of resources sooner. Here, it will be important to monitor Payrolls, JOLTS, unemployment, and Fed comments. If there is a hesitation, the multiples of hyper-valued companies will plummet. If demand recovers in Europe or China and discounts decline, we may also see further growth in share prices. Further regulatory credits could ease pressure on ASP, gross margins, and FCF. In such an environment, Tesla would be able to maintain its valuations and continue with its AI player premium.

How I See it

I don't want more fairy tales about a bright future, I want numbers. But nothing about Tesla makes sense to me. As far as I'm concerned, it's still a car manufacturer, sales are down, discounts are eating into margins, and deliveries are not on the rise. The Energy segment looks much better, but it is small compared to the automotive segment. FSD and robots are still just promises, with no results visible in the accounts, and their development is increasing costs.

The company's valuation is unrealistic. The market values Tesla as a winner in the AI segment, but so far it has only been able to deliver average numbers from the automotive segment. Moreover, the current macro environment does not help the current valuation at all. Indices are at new highs, the labor market is weakening, and inflation remains high. In such an environment, overvalued growth stocks, which Tesla definitely belongs to, fall the most. Unfortunately, I cannot take Musk's billion-dollar purchase as a signal either. If I compare it to the huge bonuses, it is just another PR stunt for me, not a change in fundamentals.

If there were real revenues from FSD or robots, or a visible jump in the Energy segment, then I might reconsider my conclusion. Until then, I will maintain my Strong Sell rating, which for me means shorting Tesla.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: New Fortress Energy: Capital Structure Arbitrage Provides 20%+ Return
data.description:
Summary

The broad difference in valuations implied by New Fortress Energy's (NASDAQ:NFE) equity valuation and the pricing of its debt instruments provides a unique trading opportunity. Investors can create a low-risk, medium-term (9-16 month) investment that provides a 20% plus return.

NFE's Current Status

The proposed transaction is essentially agnostic to New Fortress Energy's performance, so this section will be extremely brief. NFE is a highly leveraged gas-to-power provider in South America. It has existing operations in Puerto Rico, Mexico, and Brazil. Other operations are under development, but it does not appear that NFE has the resources to complete these projects given its current capital structure. Using just about any measurement of financial performance, NFE is burning cash. The company has $9 billion in debt and a market cap of less than $700 million. The company's equity valuation is inconsistent with the trading level of NFE's debt. These differences provide an attractive low-risk investment opportunity without requiring the investor to choose between the equity market's valuation and the debt market's valuation.

Investment Opportunity

NFE's debt trades as if the company is on the verge of restructuring/bankruptcy. Its largest publicly traded issue is the Senior Secured Notes of 2029 ($2.7 billion outstanding). These notes trade for approximately $15 per $100 of par value. Although these notes are not as high in the capital structure as might be assumed by their senior, secured title, the fact that they are trading at 15 reflects the debt market's view that NFE is worth far less than its outstanding debt. On the other hand, holders of its equity appear more optimistic. As a result, it's possible to lock in a low-risk, above-market return by purchasing NFE's debt and purchasing Puts on NFE's equity. Specifically, I have done the following trade:

  1. Purchase NFE 6.5% Sept 30, 2026 senior secured notes (current offer $31)
  2. Purchase NFE $1 strike Put maturing Jan 16, 2027 (current offer $0.55)
  3. For each $100 Par of notes purchased, purchase 1 Put contract (100 shares underlying)

For each $100 Par value of notes, the investor will have paid out $31 for the notes, $55 for options, and, approximately, $3 for accrued interest on the bond coupon due at the end of this month (since this amount plus slightly more will be coming to the investor in two weeks, I've chosen to ignore it in the calculations below). Day 1 investment equals $86.

Catalyst

Based upon the above information, the first hard catalyst for this trade is September 2026, when the notes mature. However, the maturity of approximately $5 billion of NFE's debt is subject to acceleration if the notes are not paid during July 2026. Thus, the first hard catalyst is July 2026. The following is from NFE's June 30, 2025 10Q (link to 10Q):

Additionally, we have $510.9 million aggregate principal amount outstanding as of June 30, 2025 under our 2026 Notes, which mature on September 30, 2026. If more than $100 million of the 2026 Notes remain outstanding 91 days prior to this maturity date (the "Springing Maturity Date"), the outstanding principal of $2.7 billion under the New 2029 Notes becomes due. If any of the 2026 Notes remains outstanding on the Springing Maturity Date, the outstanding balance under the Revolving Facility becomes due. As of June 30, 2025, the Revolving Facility was fully drawn with $710.4 million in revolving loans and $19.5 million in letters of credit. Additionally, if any of the 2026 Notes remain outstanding on July 31, 2026, the outstanding principal under the Term Loan B becomes due. Also, if any of the 2026 Notes remain outstanding 60 days prior to the maturity date of the 2026 Notes, the outstanding principal under the Term Loan A becomes due. As of June 30, 2025, there was $295.0 million outstanding under the Term Loan A and $1.27 billion outstanding under the Term Loan B.

Management's Role

Insiders own over 35% of NFE's common stock. The CEO, Wesley Edens (co-founder of Fortress Investment Group, co-owner of the Milwaukee Bucks, billionaire, etc.) owns over 10% and has purchased shares over the past year and seems inclined to try and keep the business running. The accelerated debt maturities that begin June 30, 2026, represent the proverbial fork in the road for the insiders. They can either finance/refinance the repurchase of the maturing debt (they would probably commence open market purchases and tender offers to purchase some of the debt and redeem the remainder by June 30), or they can cooperate with the debtholders through the potential reorganization and negotiate a management role and significant stock-based compensation. In either case, investors should know the direction the company is taking no later than the middle of next year.

Scenario Analysis

There are two general outcomes.

No bankruptcy, NFE pays off the 2026 Notes: Through a combination of asset sales, capital raising (driven by the insiders), and purchase of the notes prior to mid-year 2026, NFE generates sufficient capital to significantly reduce its debt outstanding. In this scenario, the 2026 Notes would be paid off in full, most likely by June 30, 2026, but possibly $100 million would remain outstanding until July 31, 2026. An investor would receive a September 2025 coupon of $3.25 per $100 face amount of notes (essentially offsetting the accrued interest paid at the time of purchase), a March coupon of $3.25, interest through June or July 2026 of between $1.62 and $2.16 (depending upon when the notes are redeemed), and would possibly capture some value via the sale of the Put options maturing in January (this number could be fairly significant). If the options are worth $0.05, they would add $5 to the total outcome. In this scenario, the initial investment of $86 is worth between $104.87 and $105.31, plus the value of the Put options (out of sheer laziness, I have not taken into account that the interim March coupon has a future value that is slightly greater than $3.25). 9-10 month absolute return (excluding value of Put options) of 22%.

NFE does not pay off the 2026 notes by mid-year 2026, and debt maturities are accelerated, and NFE files for bankruptcy. If NFE is not able to structure a solution by July, it could be forced to file for bankruptcy. Indeed, depending upon management's assessment of the situation, it may choose to file prior to the deadline. If management believes that the current capital structure is not sustainable, they may be best served by cooperating with debtholders in creating a post-bankruptcy business that is viable. Management could essentially be less confrontational in exchange for a continued management role and compensation in the new entity that is heavily incentive-based. As is the case in the above scenario, in this scenario, an investor would receive a September 2025 coupon of $3.25 per $100 face amount of notes (essentially offsetting the accrued interest paid at the time of purchase) and a March 2026 coupon of $3.25. However, any interest accrued post-March would likely be capitalized and treated as debt in bankruptcy. Assuming the reorganization is completed by January 2027, an investor would also receive a $100 payout from the Put options. It's also likely that the notes will have some positive value in bankruptcy. The price of the 2029 notes is a rough indication of what the bond market thinks is the value of the 2026 notes in bankruptcy. These notes are currently trading around 15. In this scenario, the initial investment of $86 pays out $100 (from the Puts) plus $3.25 (from the coupon received in March) plus $5 to $15 (the value of the debt in bankruptcy), a total of $108.25 to $118.25. 16-month absolute return of 25-37%

Risk

The primary risk in the above strategy is that the company commences bankruptcy (i.e., does not pay off the 2026 notes), but the reorganization is not completed by January 2027. If that happens, and the stock continues to trade post the maturity of the Put options in January 2027, the Put options will not be worth the full $1. In that scenario, the investor will either realize a smaller gain or a loss or can exercise the Puts and maintain a short stock position until the reorganization is completed.

Conclusion

NFE investors are highly focused on "picking a side." Will the company survive? Will it be forced to file for bankruptcy? Which "side" is right? However, I believe it is possible to create an investment structure that is agnostic to these questions.

data.title: ProPetro Set To Stand Out Vs. Frac Peers With Growth, Best In Show Balance Sheet
data.description:
The ProPetro Holding Corp.'s (NYSE:PUMP) second quarter results were covered on our site. It was not their best quarter, but that's well factored into the shares now. In a nutshell, they:

  • Reported a 2% revenue miss and a 17% EBITDA miss.
    • Average frac fleet count was as expected.
    • Total revenues were down 9% both sequentially and YoY.
    • Frac prices were lower than expected, and non-frac business (wireline and cementing) slipped sequentially as well.
    • EBITDA margins of 15.1% were light of expectations of 18%.
  • Eased back on completion business capex while maintaining their power segment spending plans,
    • This was the second reduction for frac capex this year.
    • Original total capex guidance range was $300 to $400 mm and is now down to $270 to $310 mm.
      • The reduction is largely reduced maintenance capex for frac.
  • And guided Q3 frac fleets to a new low for 2025 of 11 to 12 spreads vs. 13 to 14 in Q2 '25 and 14 to 15 in Q1 '25.
  • They remain 100% Permian focused with a 75% frac, 15% wireline, 10% cementing revenue mix.
    • Power segment to contribute for the first time with the Q3 '25 financials.

Balance Sheet

  • We see balance sheet strength and simplicity of capital structure in the public pressure pumping space as key, even in good times. These are not good times for the space, so it's more important now to have a clean balance sheet.
  • PUMP has the strongest balance sheet in the group.
    • Net debt plus lease liabilities of $90 mm at Q2 vs $75 mm in cash for a net debt+LL of < 0.1x annualized Q2 and TTM EBITDA.
    • As of Q2 2025, they had $45 mm on the revolver, $45 mm in lease liabilities, and no senior debt, and no preferred stock.
  • As they build out the Power segment look for leverage to creep modestly higher. To date, equipment loan rates have been < 8%.

Power Segment: (PROPWR) - formed Q4 2024 to service oil and gas producers as well as industrial projects and data center applications (ordering equipment, not yet generating revenue as of Q2 2025)

  • 220 MW on order as of last update.
    • This will be a mix of gas reciprocating engines and smaller gas turbines.
    • They can add capacity faster than large gas turbines that are now largely booked for deliveries into late 2027.
  • Deliveries started in Q3 2025 (expect an update with the Q3 report), with all capacity arriving by mid 2026.
  • Contracts to initially target Oil & Gas operations and midstream before getting into industrial and data center applications.
    • In May they announced a 10-year contract for 80 MW with a Permian-focused E&P operator.
      • This is a turnkey deal for equipment, operations, maintenance, and a take-or-pay guarantee.
    • They have an LOI with another customer.
    • Future contract terms are expected to be 10 to 20 years. Maybe some will be five year deals, but we don't expect them to do any short term, rental-type deals.
  • They expect a majority (150 to 200 MW) to be online at the start of 2026.
    • Model at $0.3 mm EBITDA per MW per year (on the low end, ranging as high as $0.4 mm/MW/yr).
    • 2026 contribution is expected to be approximately $45 mm to $60 mm just for the equipment in early 2026. This excludes any incremental orders, which we expect but have not yet been reported, as well as equipment on order and not yet in service by early 2026.
  • Before year end 2025:
    • We expect to see another long-term turnkey generation deal(s) announced.
    • We may also see an increase in capacity on order.

Return of capital:

  • No dividend.
  • Repurchase program:
    • $200 mm program in place through December of 2026 with $89 mm remaining authorized.
    • Active in 2024 but inactive for the last 3 quarters as conditions in frac deteriorated and Power became the focus.

Other Items:

  • Ownership
    • Exxon holds 16.6 million shares in PUMP after their acquisition of PXD.
      • Revenues from Exxon were 20% and 22% of the total in Q1 2025 and Q2 2025.
      • Exxon was also 28% of accounts receivable at mid year.
      • Our sense is Exxon (and subsidiary XTO) lends confidence to their frac portfolio during down times but probably weighs on ability to gain pricing increases during better times.
    • The top 10 holders here have 68% of shares (Exxon, BlackRock, Sourcerock, Vanguard, etc.)
  • Float: 79 mm shares (76% of outstanding)
  • Short interest: 15% of float is short.
    • 2nd highest in the group after ACDC, which has balance sheet troubles.
    • Our sense has been that the singular focus on the Permian has prompted shorts to target them as the Permian rig count has come under pressure and as active frac spreads are said to have contracted in the basin as well.
    • We see this position as in need of covering as sentiment improves and as the Power segment begins to generate cash flow.

Nutshell: EBITDA Growth Is Expected in 2026 and Cheapest in Group

  • This is trough-level frac, wireline, and cementing contributions plus PROPWR.
  • They are the only frac name where positive EBITDA growth is expected in 2026.
  • At current levels they are the cheapest name in the group despite having the strongest balance sheet (see FRAC Comp below the PUMP cheat sheet below).
  • We own shares of PUMP in our INVEST and FLEX portfolios.

data.title: Mobius' Favorite Global Markets For 2025-2026
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, emerging markets legend Dr. Mark Mobius shares his outlook on global markets, from Asia’s top growth engines to Latin America’s reform stories.

Transcript

Mike Larson: The Godfather of Emerging Markets. That's the nickname this week's guest on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters podcast is known by. Why? He has visited more than 110 countries and traveled more than a million miles doing so. He's invested in more than 5,000 companies throughout his long career. He's written a dozen books on investing in economics, and he is one of the world's foremost experts on investing in emerging and frontier markets. And I couldn't think of a better time to have this guest on.

For one thing, many foreign markets have vastly outperformed the US S&P 500 in 2025. For another, shifts in global trade and tariff policy are leading US and global investors to re-evaluate their asset allocations and geographic exposure. Countries like China, India, Taiwan, Japan, and even Argentina are offering up new investing opportunities, but also introducing new potential risks into investor portfolios. So, without further ado, let me introduce the guest. He's Dr. Mark Mobius, Chairman of the Mobius Emerging Opportunities Fund. Mark, welcome to the program.

Mark Mobius: Pleasure being with you.

Mike Larson: I know that you're in Dubai. You've obviously been traveling quite a bit in the last year or two and looking at opportunities across the globe in this environment. So, I wanted to start with sort of your big picture view of where US and foreign markets stand here, and what are some of the things that have been driving noteworthy -- on a noteworthy point, foreign market outperformance of US equities?

Mark Mobius: Well, Japan is one of the interesting places. In fact, I was just there a few weeks ago and visiting some companies and there's some excellent companies in Japan that are performing well and still very profitable, not very expensive. So, I would say Japan is a place where you want to look.

China is coming back, believe it or not. I mean, I was a big fan of India for so long, I still am, but China is outperforming India by a pretty wide margin. And I think the reason for that may be a loosening up by the Communist Party. I think Xi Jinping is loosening up his hardline stance for the local economy because he realizes that he's got to promote the domestic economy; otherwise, it'll be a real political problem. So, China looks very, very good.

And then we return to Southeast Asia. Probably the most noteworthy place would be now Vietnam and Thailand to some extent, but Vietnam is the more dynamic of the economies in that part of the world. So, those are the places I'd be. Of course, we can't forget Taiwan. Taiwan is an incredible place to invest. We're finding very, very good bargains in Taiwan. One of our important criteria is return on capital. And Taiwan companies are very, very good in that regard. So, that's another place you want to look.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. I definitely want to walk through essentially each of those countries in the investment case for our stock space there. But I do want to start here in the US. Many of our listeners and viewers will be watching from here in the States. And I'm curious, so much of the media coverage is about tariffs, it's about trade, it's about an America-first type policy. And I'm curious as to your thoughts on what impacts you see that having on global investing and the US and foreign economies.

Mark Mobius: Well, it's definitely having an impact depending on which country you're talking to. India would be a good example now with the 25 plus 25 tariff put on the Indian exports to the US. And also the recent decision to tack $100,000 on to the people who want to get visas. These are the high-level software engineers that the US is using a lot from India. That's going to have an impact.

But even in India, if you look at the total picture, yes, jewelry, high-end gold jewelry is being hit very hard because of the import tariffs and the consumer goods, garments, that sort of thing being hit, but all other sectors are doing fine. So, it's a huge country, they have a huge domestic market and they're beginning to now deregulate the economy, which is so important because it's quite a bureaucracy in India. They have to deregulate. And as they do that, it's going to be very good for the economy.

Mike Larson: Why do you think investors have been able to sort of make peace with the issue of trade and tariffs after obviously all the turmoil we saw in the first quarter and rolling into April of this year?

Mark Mobius: I think they realized that it's -- you got to use a rifle instead of a shotgun. In other words, you got to look at individual cases and where the tariffs will hit and where it will not hit. For example, if you look at some American companies, there are many American companies that do not depend on imports and exports sales overseas. So, those are the companies that will do fine. But even those that are hit with tariffs, they're finding ways to get around these problems with automation, local manufacturing, et cetera, et cetera.

So, one of the things I've noticed, as you know, I'm known for emerging markets. And I now use a new term called emerging opportunities. Because if you look at the US market and the number of companies that depend on emerging countries for their sales or their supplies, you realize that if you buy into the American market, you're going to get a lot of exposure to emerging countries.

So, I think this -- the adjustments regarding the tariffs will take place. They're taking place as we speak. People will adjust to it and life will go on. It's not going to be a big tragedy.

Mike Larson: So, let's walk through country by country, some of the names you mentioned earlier. China obviously being the biggest. Do you think that some of what the party's doing over there is improving the economic backdrop and obviously the trade issue? So, speak to the investment case there.

Mark Mobius: Yeah, as you know, when Xi Jinping came in, he wanted to go back to the old Mao period, where the state was leader of the economy. In other words, state-owned companies would take over private enterprises, or at least state-owned enterprises would be leading the economy. Of course, that's a deadly prescription for such a dynamic society as you have in China. So, that slowed down the economy. And then, of course, you had the property problem with a lot of people losing their life savings. They invested in property.

So, these two things really woke up the Communist Party, I believe. And they realized that they've got to change. They've got to allow more private enterprise to come up. And you notice Alibaba (BABA) and companies like that are now coming back and you see more and more of them being more active and I believe that's a definite policy by the communist government to do that in order to revive the economy. And as that happens, of course, stock market is doing well. We've seen it outperforming.

Mike Larson: Okay. Taiwan, of course, there's always the saber rattling in the background of what China might do with Taiwan, what the US might do in response on the geopolitical front. What is the investment case there, and how does that factor -- those geopolitical issues factor here into any of your investing decisions?

Mark Mobius: Well, first of all, I don't think China will take a hard military action against Taiwan. In other words, it will not be a hot war. I believe they will continue to wear away at the propaganda. In other words, they're going to keep on convincing people that Taiwan is part of China and they should join the Chinese government. And that may come to pass in the next decade because China is changing, and they may become closer and closer to the kind of structure -- government structure that Taiwan has with multiple parties, political parties. I know it sounds far-fetched at this stage, but I believe they're going to do that.

So, at the end of the day, I don't worry about any military action against Taiwan. Now, it could happen, but I don't think it makes sense to dwell on that. What you've got to look at is the incredible dynamism of the Taiwan companies and the entrepreneurs. The economy is doing very well. And as you know, the tech industry is really first rate and TSMC (TSM), companies like that are doing very well globally and including China, they're selling a lot to China. So, I think Taiwan is really going to do very well.

Mike Larson: Let's pivot to Japan. You mentioned, obviously, again, when we were talking off camera, you'd been there for a couple of months. You posted some fascinating information on your blog there, your visit to Osaka, and seeing the Japanese sumo wrestlers train. Found that a very interesting read. What is your big picture thought on investing in Japan? Why is that a favorable environment after so many years where investors seem to avoid the country?

Mark Mobius: Yes. I mean, for a long, long time, the big Zaibatsu, the companies like the Mitsui (OTCPK:MITSY), the Mitsubishi (OTCPK:MSBHF), these companies were not that profitable. I mean, they had incredible assets, but they were not growing that fast and they were not that profitable. I think now they are beginning to realize that maybe they should begin to break up some of these companies and take advantage of the incredible opportunities in the individual components. I mean, they're in mining, they're in industry, they're in consumer goods, they're in all these things, which could be spun off. And I think gradually that could happen in Japan, which would be very, very exciting.

What interested us in Japan was the consulting business and the move towards a more and more AI-oriented consulting and reorganization. And we found a few companies in Japan with very good earnings, very profitable, that were involved in enterprise consulting. So, I think that's an area which deserves more attention. But as I said, some of these big conglomerates may be worth looking at again.

Mike Larson: Another country I wanted to give you a chance to speak about was Vietnam. I know you talked in recent posts about finding some interesting opportunities there. What are your thoughts on that country and Southeast Asia, ex-China for that matter?

Mark Mobius: Well, first of all, the Vietnamese are very dynamic people, very intelligent, very ambitious. And you must remember there's North and South. The South is more enterprising, as you might expect. But the North, although they're running the show in terms of politics, they're allowing the South to have more and more freedom to do enterprises and to open up more and more markets globally.

The thing about Vietnam is that they've learned a lot from China. The Chinese companies have gone to Vietnam to manufacture in order to get into the US market sort of through the back door. And many of these companies are not just doing this for show, but they're really actually doing manufacturing from soup to nuts. They're going right through the entire economy. And that's very exciting in Taiwan -- in Taiwan and also in Vietnam. You see this enterprise system, which is very exciting and they're learning a lot from the Chinese and from the Taiwanese and other nations that have been exporting globally.

Mike Larson: You did already touch on it somewhat, but I did want to pivot to India next. I mean, when I look at, again, for US-based investor, many of the ETFs in some of these foreign countries, Taiwan's done very well, Vietnam's done very well, along with Japan, whereas something like the PIN ETF, which allows you to access India as a US-based investor, is essentially flat on the year. How do you think the country will deal with the challenges, whether it's the H-1B question or whether it's some of the other trade tensions with the US?

Mark Mobius: I think what they're going to do is continue negotiating with the US. They're very, very good negotiators. But more importantly, they're going to start a reforms program in the country. You must remember, I like to consider India as the United States of India because you have all these different states with different languages, both written and verbal languages. So, it's very difficult to unify a country like this. And this is what they've been working on all these years.

But now they’re beginning to wake up to the fact that they've got to loosen up. They've got to get rid of some of these onerous regulations which holds back the enterprises, particularly the smaller enterprises. And I think the Prime Minister has done a good job moving in that direction, but it's not easy. It's going to take time. But now with what's happening with the US and the tariffs and the restrictions, it's been put on the burner. They're now moving ahead much faster to do that, and that'll be excellent for the Indian economy.

Mike Larson: We haven't talked really about South America yet. But since Argentina is in the news, I figured I'd have to ask you about the latest issues there and a potential US backstop to help the currency and the markets down there. Any thoughts on countries like Argentina or some of these other names that you maybe have mentioned in the past as investment opportunities or is that something you're looking to kind of avoid for now?

Mark Mobius: No, Argentina has had an incredible success. It's really amazing. I must admit I was a little late. I didn't believe what they were doing because, we've been disappointed very often in the past, but now I see they really made some terrific reforms. What's most exciting about Argentina is the demonstration effect it will have on other countries in Latin America.

I think other countries will begin to look at Argentina and say, hey, maybe we should be following these kinds of policies. And that would be truly revolutionary. That would be a wonderful thing to see, but it remains to be seen. And nevertheless, there are opportunities, individual company opportunities in Brazil, in Argentina, Chile, Mexico, Colombia. Colombia is a very dynamic group of entrepreneurs. So, there are quite a few opportunities there.

Mike Larson: Okay. I guess at this point, we still have some time, but I want to make sure if there are and if there is a name or two or three, or maybe whether it's an ETF, individual company, some of the names that you're eyeing in a few of the countries we've talked about. I'd love to hear about them. I know you briefly mentioned Taiwan Semiconductor, for example.

Mark Mobius: Yeah, I can't give you individual names we're in the process of buying.

Mike Larson: Fair enough.

Mark Mobius: But I must recommend ETFs. We do have ETFs in our portfolio to give us exposure to countries and sectors by the way. For example, one ETF which is quite interesting is that defense, European Defense ETF. So, that's one that I think people should look at. And some of the country ETFs you mentioned, some of them have been doing very well. China is doing very well. India, not so much. So, we lightened up on India. So, I would look at both the country and the sector. There are a number of very nice ETFs in the technology sector that you can buy.

Mike Larson: Let's talk about the technology sector. I mean, obviously, some of that is a US play, but those many tech companies have extreme exposure overseas as well. What are your thoughts on AI? Everybody is asking about it and what's going on in that part of the market? Do you think it's boom, bubble, something you're worried about or not?

Mark Mobius: I think people have got to realize that AI is simply information speeded up at the speed of light. That's basically -- that's what AI is. It's information that is utilized by people. And we have found AI to be really helpful in our analysis of companies, countries and so forth. Because the information is at your fingertips, within a few minutes you get all this information. So it's terrific. And it's impacting every industry, whether it be banking, whether it be our industry, investment management, industry of any type. So I think AI -- we must remember what it is. It's not something that's revolutionary in that sense. It's something that is -- it's a speeding up of the information flow. And it's also covering a wider range of information globally. And that's really exciting. So yeah, we're using AI and I think more and more companies will be using AI, but it won't be -- at the end of the day, it won't be the handle in decision-making. It'll help us make decisions, but it won't be the -- it won’t make decisions for us. Maybe we shouldn't allow it to make decisions.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. No, I understand completely. Certainly I use AI as a tool in my job as well. Since you brought up Europe, I think it's an interesting dynamic that these countries are going to be spending more apparently on defense as part of some of the shifts the US has done, and certainly to counter some of the threats from Russia. Thoughts on how that's going to evolve and what that means for economies there in the investment case for Europe overall?

Mark Mobius: Well, Europe, as I mentioned, good idea to buy a defense ETF. The ETF of companies that make armaments because they're spending more and more. I think that it's a stalemate in Europe and it will remain that way. Eventually both sides, Russia and Ukraine will tire and it'll wind down. There'll be sporadic engagements, but generally speaking, it'll wind down because the US -- Trump is not interested in pouring more money into Ukraine. And I think Russia is also beginning to tire. It’s had a big impact on the Russian economy. So I'm not too worried about that situation.

Mike Larson: Yeah, fair enough. I know I'm going to go to Charlotte and see if there's any questions in a minute, but I do want to just given the theme of this event, talk to you a little bit about precious metals and what's happening sort of in the global monetary system. Clearly an incredible rally in gold, silver has been following now, we've seen other metals, platinum and so on begin to play catch up. What are your thoughts on what's driving that and how sustainable do you think that trend is as we get closer to 2026?

Mark Mobius: It's a very strong trend that is related to money supply growth. If you look at the money supply growth of the US dollar, you'll see it's turning up again. During COVID, of course, it went through the roof, came down, now it's going up again. And there's a whole new generation of young people who believe that the US dollar is not going to be worth anything in the future. And so, they're switching to crypto and to gold. I have some young friends, analysts who were with me, who only have faith in crypto and gold. So I think there's a wide ranging trend in that direction. More and more people are going to be buying gold. And therefore the gold prices line up a lot. However, I would recommend people to put stop loss limits on their gold. Don't get too carried away. And by the way, that's true of all the stocks, the US market has done so well, it's surged to such a high level, it's probably a good idea to protect yourself from the downside, put stop loss limits on the stocks and ETFs that you - and gold.

Mike Larson: Okay. Yeah, that's a great point. I mean, I think a lot of people kind of came into this August, September period worried about seasonality. Certainly, I saw a lot of commentary on that. And we've defied a lot of skeptics in the US market. When it comes to the gold versus crypto debate, any thoughts that you'd have on which side you'd favor or is it more of a both type environment for those kinds of assets?

Mark Mobius: For me, I favor gold. However, I would want to go into a crypto exchange as a stock. That might be an interesting investment. But generally speaking, I'm more -- I believe gold is a better investment.

Mike Larson: Okay. Charlotte, I wanted to go ahead and give you a chance. Were there any questions that you had in the queue that you want to read off so Mark can address them?

Charlotte: Yeah, just one quick question. William Adams was wondering, what is the ticker for the European defense ETF?

Mark Mobius: Defense ETFs, a good idea, I think. I haven't looked at those ETFs yet, but I know the European ones are doing quite well. And it probably makes sense to have something in the US defense ETFs. Because let’s remember, Trump is not reducing the expenditure on defense. So that will continue moving up. So yeah, it's a good idea to get a defense. I haven't looked at the pricing, so don't take my word for it. You better look at the pricing and so forth. But generally speaking, I'm in favor of that.

Mike Larson: Okay, fair enough. What else did you have there Charlotte at the moment?

Charlotte: Seems like we're all caught up on questions for now. Just that one from William, but I'll let you know if we get anymore.

Mike Larson: Okay, sure. That sounds good. Mark, I guess I want to ask, again, as we get kind of towards the end of the year, 2026 is approaching. Are there any big themes, big threats, I guess I would even ask that you think investors are going to have to face up to or look to capitalize on in the New Year versus maybe what's been working in 2025?

Mark Mobius: I think the big story will be about China. China is a huge economy and any political moves in China against the trend towards more liberalization will not be good news for China or for the rest of the world. So that's one thing we have to look at. And then India, India is going to become the largest economy in the world. They're now about 1.2 billion people compared to 800 million in China. Average age in India is about 27. China is about 37, 38. So India is going to be a real powerhouse going forward. So I think you have to watch what's happening in India as well. So I would say those are the two big trends going forward. And of course, the degree to which US companies can take advantage of those trends will be very important.

Mike Larson: And I guess that brings up an interesting point. I mean, outside of say technology, are there other US-based sectors or names, ETFs that you think are good ways to -- if you're a US investor who wants to keep most of your money here, but still access growth overseas through sales that the companies do overseas. Any other sectors that you think would be worth targeting?

Mark Mobius: Yeah, I would say mining, North American mining, minerals and mining. And North America might be a good thing to look at. We have some companies in that sector. And of course, with commodities generally moving up, companies in the mining sector might make sense. I'm not saying that it should be gold, but any mining company of that nature. And the second area would be in agriculture. I think you got to look at the companies in agriculture because the US will continue to do very well in that sector. And of course, with Trump trying to open up global markets, exports will probably surge. It will be very good for the American farmer.

Mike Larson: Okay. One thing I realized we probably haven't touched on, but I think we should in the time we have left would be from a policy front, monetary policy, fiscal policy. Obviously, the Fed has started at least once to cut rates again. I'm wondering how you think the path of rates going forward is as we roll towards the New Year, how much you think some of this political pressure will or will not manifest itself in that arena?

Mark Mobius: Well, I think you should get rid of the Fed. The Fed should be closed down, because they've been responsible for all these problems in the economy. Too much money supply, then turn down the money supply, you have these up and down in the economy. So unfortunately, we are stuck with the Fed, but hopefully there'll be some reform. And the best thing that could happen, and it may happen now under Trump, the Fed is given only one task, and that is to maintain stability of the currency. See now they have two tasks. They have two objectives. One, maintain this currency stability and two, to help the economy. You can't do these two things at the same time. It's impossible. So hopefully there'll be some reform. And that's -- I think the young generation are now beginning to realize the harm that the Fed has done. So -- but nevertheless, we got to live with it. And I think you're going to see continuing reduction in the interest rates going forward next year, mainly because of Trump's determination to get those interest rates down.

Mike Larson: Do you think then as a follow on that that will give more impetus to the trend in the dollar that we've seen, clearly the worst first half of the year on a broad base since 1973, I think it was. We've seen some stabilization there, but moving forward, how do you think the dollar is going to react to this environment that we have?

Mark Mobius: I think the dollar will maintain its strength. It's come down against some -- you must remember, some currencies have weakened against the US dollar. Some have strengthened, so it's a mixed bag. The general index of US dollar has come down, but that's mainly influenced by the euro. The euro has been quite strong, but I don't recommend going to the euro at crazy low interest rates that they have. So I think the dollar will continue to be the leading currency in the world, despite the fact that China wants to change it, wants to get rid of it, wants to do that. The only way that China could do that would be to put the renminbi on gold, the gold standard, which is possible because they've been collecting a lot of gold. China, I think, is now the largest producer of gold, mining gold, and they're buying gold. So that may happen, but I don't see in the short term that we have to worry about that.

Mike Larson: Okay. I understand. Charlotte, we do have a couple more questions in the queue. So I'll hand it over to her to read one of them off.

Charlotte: All right. Fantastic. Thank you. Starting with this one from Charles. He was just asking, are there countries where government bonds would represent a good opportunity, in your opinion at least?

Mark Mobius: I'm not too keen on bonds other than US bonds, because you've got the risk of currency, you've got the risk of interest rates, you've got political risk. So I would not be too excited about bonds. And I must admit, I'm not the expert in that area, but I -- generally speaking, I'd stay away from bonds.

Charlotte: All right, thank you. The next question is from Les. He's asking, in the next six months, are there any foreign markets that we should watch for possible upside trading?

Mark Mobius: Yeah, China would be number one, because that's begun already. I think there could be a reversal in what's happening in India, but we have to watch that. I wouldn't recommend now, but I would watch that carefully because the Indian economy is so dynamic, you can have something happening there. And maybe Mexico is another place you want to look. And Taiwan, as I mentioned Taiwan.

Mike Larson: Okay, so we've got China, India, Mexico, and Taiwan as opportunities there.

Mark Mobius: Right.

Charlotte: And Eddie also asked, what about Israel?

Mark Mobius: Israel is an incredible country. So many smart people. So they're doing so well in technology. So it's well worth looking at Israeli companies and particularly companies that have operations globally -- global operations. You're going to find some terrific companies. So yes, I would definitely look at Israel.

Charlotte: All right. And one final question for you. This one is from Craig. They're asking about Vietnam. Do you have any recommendations on Vietnam?

Mark Mobius: Yeah, I would look at ETFs, the Vietnam ETFs, and I would also look at some of the property companies in Vietnam. Those are the two that come to mind at this stage.

Mike Larson: Okay. Was that the last one you had there, Charlotte?

Charlotte: That is everything. Thank you.

Mike Larson: Okay. Mark, we have a last couple of minutes here. So I just -- I wanted to ask if there's anything that we haven't covered that you think it's important for investors to take away here. I know essentially if I had to summarize what you were saying, cautious on the stock market, maybe after a big run. You talked about some of these individual foreign countries and a few sectors, but again, this is sort of a summary, I guess I would ask if there's anything that we haven't covered, anything you think is most important, is your message for investors watching this.

Mark Mobius: Well, I think you have to look at the Trump administration very carefully and listen to what they're saying, watch what they're doing, because they're having a global impact. We must remember it's not only about US policy, but the Trump administration is having a big influence on countries around the world. So that's -- I would keep a close eye on that. Of course, be careful. There's a lot of false information out there, but look at what they're doing and listen to what they're saying in a very objective way. Because that will give you a lot of guidance on which way to move going forward.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. Well, Dr. Mobius, I do appreciate you taking time out chat here from Dubai. I hope you have safe travels. We really appreciate the perspective and questions from the audience. Appreciate all of that as well. Be ready for a full day of education here on gold, crypto and markets today and tomorrow as well. So thank you again.

Mark Mobius: Thank you. Bye-bye.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Micron, Alibaba, Nvidia, AI Theme Maturing
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia's new deal, AI theme maturing and separate from broader market (0:15). Micron earnings, Alibaba's aggressive spending (2:45). Klarna, Affirm, and PayPal (6:00). Starbucks closures (9:45). Jobs data and elevated inflation (13:30).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's Director of news. A lot going on in tech. No surprise, I suppose. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) doing deals all over the place. Is that top of the list this week?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, I think we have to think of AI and the companies that are major names there as a pocket market within the larger market.

And talk about it separately because the dynamics within that market are a little separate than the broader market that includes most of the companies that we're talking about. So I think it's been an interesting week in the AI market.

I mean, we've been talking about for a while now how the AI boom is no longer a burgeoning topic. It's now a mature theme that's driving the overall stock market.

The way that companies are responding to that is pretty interesting. So the specific news that you're alluding to, NVIDIA signed a deal with OpenAI. They're going to invest up to $100,000,000,000 in OpenAI over a period of time. The first part of that is gonna be 10,000,000,000 which values OpenAI at 500,000,000,000.

As part of it, basically this money is gonna be used to build out OpenAI's AI infrastructure, and part of the deal is that money is gonna go back to NVIDIA in the form of purchases of their chips.

So, basically, getting an investment in OpenAI, and OpenAI is gonna use that money to buy NVIDIA chips partly to help build out their processing centers.

I think it's interesting to note in its last earnings report, NVIDIA had $57,000,000,000 of cash on hand. Since then, we've heard about this OpenAI deal, also the Intel (INTC) deal, which was $5,000,000,000 that NVIDIA was investing in Intel.

So I think this is a sign that NVIDIA is using the windfall from the early stages of the AI boom to put its tentacles all over the industry to get involved in other parts of it, center itself as more than just the chip provider and more of just a general AI hub.

Oracle (ORCL), you can see doing a similar strategy where they're using this opportunity to invest in other companies and be part of other projects.

So as I said, I think it's just an interesting sign of how companies are are taking the next step as the AI process evolves over time.

RS: And what else would you point to in that space? Micron (NASDAQ:MU) earnings, what else would you provide in terms of what's happening in that part of the market?

BS: I think Micron is an interesting case study. It's interesting to take Micron in contrast with Alibaba (NYSE:BABA). Both companies had news this week.

For Micron, reported earnings, really good report, beat expectations, strong guidance, revenue up 46% year over year. That included 69% growth in its DRAM, memory chip market, but the stock fell after the report, not dramatically, but drifted lower the day after and then is lower again today.

So you're seeing investors kind of shrug. The stock is up 86% year over year. It reached a fifty two week high heading into the earnings report. So you saw a lot of optimism going into it and you end up with a sell on the news response there.

In that case, people are taking a wait and see attitude whether or not Micron can justify the valuation it currently has by executing on the promise that it has.

Meanwhile, I see Alibaba then filled a new AI model. Also said it would get more aggressive about its AI spending, building out its AI capabilities. It rose 8% on that news, hitting a new high. It's up about a 100% year to date.

So that's a company that got a lot of love from the stock market for making its investment in AI more underlining it and putting a spotlight on it whereas Micron took a little step back after its news.

Now part of it might just be because the earnings was on the calendar, and people had bid up the stock heading into that, whereas Alibaba was more of an out of the blue announcement.

So it's interesting to see how the market responds to the various news items we saw in previous weeks, companies getting large contracts, that getting a huge up in the stock price.

But then you see companies report earnings and shrug afterwards. So it's really a promise versus execution situation now.

RS: Anything to say about the possible news that Intel and Apple (AAPL) are doing a deal together as long as we're talking this theme?

BS: As you point out, I think that just plays into the general theme where these companies are partnering up a lot. Intel has been at the center of a lot of deals lately.

That's just a sign that Intel missed the boat the first time out and is propelling its turnaround by getting involved with some of these other companies.

So like you're pointing out, I think that just plays into the general theme that we're seeing now as we're in the whatever stage you wanna call it, second, third, whatever stage of the AI build out. You're seeing companies look around at where they can find partnerships and where they can find space for further growth in a highly competitive market.

RS: Always appreciate when somebody does not mix their metaphor so really appreciate you keeping that consistent with the boat, the propeller, and nobody can accuse us of inconsistent metaphors.

What else is going on in the market? We talked some IPO news either last week or the week before that with Klarna (KLAR). Are are you seeing more news out of them? What else are you looking at?

BS: I think Klarna is an interesting stock to check-in on, and the buy now pay later space in general. Affirm (AFRM), which is a competitor of Klarna, was down 16% over the past week. No major news out of them, just sort of a drift lower.

Meanwhile, Klarna is down 8% over the past week as well. Just before we started recording, it was trading just above $39 per share. It came public at $40 per share.

So if it holds at these levels, it's gonna close below its IPO price for the first time. The stock saw a pop when it first came out, like you said, a couple weeks ago. It reached $57.20 per share and then closed its first day at $45.82. That $45.82 is still the highest close for the stock. It's been a general attrition ever since that early enthusiasm.

There was another piece of news that I thought was kind of interesting from the space. I don't wanna make too much of a big deal of it, but it did make my radar.

PayPal (PYPL) signed a deal to sell buy now, pay later receivables to Blue Owl Capital, so it's selling $7,000,000,000 of those receivables.

I thought it was kind of interesting that PayPal was lowering its exposure to these kinds of receivables at the same time that other more pure play members of that sector are heading lower.

I don't know. It could be a little bit of a red flag, and these are companies that are potentially very economically sensitive if there's an uptick in late payments, people defaulting on the loans that they've made, it could have a big impact.

This is a relatively new space, and we haven't really seen them go through tough economic situation. So this muddy economic scenario that we're facing right now might be the first real test for these kinds of companies.

RS: Can you explain a little bit more about what the deal is at PayPal in a little bit more detail for those who may not know what the business model is or what the receivables are or what that deal means for PayPal?

BS: PayPal has a buy now, pay later option. I think it's buy in four. So, basically, it splits up whatever you're buying into four chunks and you pay, I think, monthly. And so it operates very similar to the types of products that Affirm has or Klarna, the company is loaning you this money so that you can buy more with less income.

They might not have the kind of credit limit that they need to make either this particular purchase or maybe they're maxed out so the credit card isn't really an option or maybe the interest rate on the credit card is so high that this deal makes sense.

And so what PayPal's doing is they have this money on the books, this money that they've lent out in this, in this product, and they're selling a part of it to an alternative investment company. So basically getting it off their books.

This could just be a way to clean up the balance sheet a little bit. I don't wanna overstate the importance of this for the buy now pay later industry, but I did think it was interesting that PayPal, which has a more diversified business model than say a Klarna or an Affirm.

I think it's interesting that PayPal had decided to limit its exposure to this a little bit. So it should be underlined that it didn't shut down the product or anything. It's no longer doing this. It's just moving some of this debt off of its books and giving it to somebody else.

RS: Appreciate that. Any other sectors to highlight? We've talked retail in the past. I saw some headlines about Starbucks (SBUX) closing some stores and doing some layoffs.

You know, I have to say when I see headlines like that, I'm always curious because it's obvious that Starbucks has had multiple growth phases where they're exponentially opening stores only to close them a bit later.

And I'm always curious about the initial thought process there. Thoughts on what's happening there or things to say about the retail sector these days?

BS: Yeah. They definitely have a tendency to do sort of an accordion model. Where you pull it out and then you push it back in a little bit.

RS: Is there a method to that madness? Is it smarter than I think it is?

BS: I can't speak to Starbucks specifically. I know that there were retailers who were taking advantage of cheap mall space. Malls would need people in it, so they would get a free month and incentives to move into a space.

And so there there were times when specific mall retailers would just have these sort of expansion binges where they're taking advantage of this cheap real estate.

So I don't know if there's factors like that that play into Starbucks. I do know there are things - when you see Starbucks on both sides of the same street, there's a reason for that because they want people to be able to turn right into the drive through and right out of it. So you're creating a situation where people don't have to stop traffic to pull into the Starbucks.

Some things that seem sort of absurd in Starbucks' model from the outside actually make a lot of sense when you look at the logistics of it. This particular closing was characterized by the company as them getting back to Starbucks model, part of their turnaround.

So they're closing stores that don't have that coffee shop vibe that they're trying to to get back to. Who knows how real that is, whether that's the actual motivation behind it, whether it was looking at the physical space and saying this doesn't fit our brand anymore, or if it was these are underperforming stores, and now we're just gonna use this this as an excuse.

You always wanna take corporate communications with a grain of salt.

They did combine closing these stores with job cuts at their main headquarters staff. Support staff is being trimmed as well. So it seems like a little bit more than just a rebranding effort.
