I thought it was interesting, to kind of shift to a different topic. With the potential government shutdown coming, which could involve, however temporary, a massive amount of jobs being being cut.

We have, I think, just seen anecdotally a tick up in layoffs in various companies moving into this. There have been signs that the labor market itself has been soft moving in to this.

So I think there's a fear that a prolonged government shutdown could be the feather that tips over the rock off the cliff kind of thing. So there's more worry around any economic signs.

I think a large company announcing any sort of closing or any sort of layoffs, no matter how much it is just rearranging deck chairs, I think it has an outsized psychological impact on investors.

RS: And what else would you point to for this week that we should be reminding investors of or looking towards next week.

By the way, we didn't have Wall Street Brunch this past week. I know that we were all missing Kim Khan's look ahead, but it will be back this week for investors' edification.

BS: Excellent. Always look forward to to whatever Kim has to say.

Looking ahead, jobs data is the main topic next week. We had the PCE come out this week. It showed pretty much what we expected, that inflation remains elevated.

Today's PCE, it's the Fed's favorite inflation gauge. It's in line with people expected, elevated but no more elevated than anyone had worried about.

The Fed already cut rates. There's no expectation that that rate cut would have any impact immediately on inflation. So the data that came out just now is extremely back looking.

I think it was largely ignored by the market, but the jobs data that's coming out next week is a little bit more real time and I think less baked in.

There have been some signs that maybe the fears about the labor market may have gotten overblown a little bit. There's been some positive signs. We saw a positive revision to GDP data. I think jobless claims were not horrible in any way.

Going into the jobless data, I think there might be slight optimism that that we might see a stabilization there, but I think that's going to set the tone as we get into October.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Growth Meets FDA Scrutiny And Tariff Risks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) stock has been on quite a bullish run. It has managed uncertainties well and was able to overcome the fallout of a partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO). Over the last month, the stock has risen by almost 20% and is now experiencing another gradual climb back to the highs of $70 we saw in late July. However, as we move deeper into H2 2025, we are seeing some execution and regulatory risks.

The company has proven to me it's able to scale rapidly, especially with subscription growth. Hims has not only managed to bring in new customers but also to upsell its existing ones. The new offerings, such as their testosterone treatments, are starting to come to fruition. Management is also clearly investing way ahead of demand, building out infrastructure and inventory, which is why we saw spending increase. I expect Hims to capture a larger share of the consumer health market as the telehealth model gains broader acceptance, especially in the U.S.

The premium valuation, however, already reflects high expectations on this part, so there is little room for missteps. FDA scrutiny of compounded GLP-1 marketing and Trump's new tariff regime seem to be giving investors a little bit of uncertainty about what had been the company's fastest-growing revenue stream in GLP-1s. Even if the direct financial hit is minimal today, the stock has seen some volatility ahead regarding this. For investors, I think Hims is a good opportunity to hold a digital health disruptor, but it's not for the faint-hearted; investing in this stock means being comfortable with volatility in the near term and being willing to wait out regulatory overhangs.

Q2 2025 Results

Last quarterly earnings were a bit of a breakout. Revenue surged 73% since the same period last year to hit $544.8 million, indicating that their new categories are gaining momentum. As I mentioned in my previous coverage, I highlighted their latest pipeline offerings in menopause and low testosterone treatments as key drivers of growth. This is a theme I continue to watch for in H2 2025. Subscriber growth remained quite robust at 31%, with active subscriptions reaching 2.44 million. While revenue per subscriber climbed roughly 30% to $74, the company isn't only adding customers but also upselling the existing ones to higher-value offerings. Someone on a hair loss treatment might be tempted to also take on a treatment for stress/anxiety, for example. We should expect customers to expand their existing dermatology lines and specialty care.

All these figures translated into substantial bottom-line growth. Net income more than tripled, and adjusted EBITDA more than doubled from the prior year, which was a welcome improvement. However, gross margin did see a bit of compression from 81% to 76%, so this could be our first hint of rising costs on their platform, among tariffs. But the compression is tied more to scaling pharmacy and telehealth operations, as well as increasing promotional spend on their new offerings to maintain momentum in a very crowded health market right now.

Despite the strong profitability metrics, I did see a red flag. We saw a swing to negative free cash flow, with a $69.4 million outflow compared to just $47.6 million in positive cash flow for the same quarter last year. The big driver here in that regard seems to be heavy investment in inventory, property, and infrastructure. The moves are consistent with management's stated strategy to build a longer-term capacity for new specialties and international expansion. Of course, the $970 million in convertible notes issued earlier this year has dramatically bolstered their liquidity despite hitting the stock with a bit of turbulence. It leaves Hims with just over $1.1 billion in cash, but it also gives significant leverage that investors will be watching for closely. Guidance for Q3 and FY 2025 suggests continued top-line momentum, though EBITDA margins are expected to moderate slightly as reinvestment continues in those areas I mentioned.

Is the FDA Closing In?

As you heard, the FDA issued about 100 warning letters to various drugmakers and telehealth providers recently. From what I remember, this is one of the agency's most aggressive crackdowns this decade on pharmaceutical advertising practices. The bad news for investors was that Hims was specifically targeted for its marketing of compounded semaglutide, with the FDA highlighting that its promotions essentially implied equivalence to FDA-approved products. Now, these products they're referring to are, of course, Novo's Wegovy and Ozempic. We saw the FDA stress that compounded versions, such as Hims' ones, are not FDA-approved, making such claims false or misleading. They went on to label the products as “misbranded.”

If you remember, compounding telehealth platforms, with compounded semaglutide, were initially permitted to produce these GLP-1 drugs during semaglutide shortages. However, with supply now stabilized, the FDA appears to be quite intent on eliminating marketing that blurs the line between compounded and branded drugs. You see, the majority of the population won't see the difference between compounded and non-compounded drugs at first; they'll only see the difference in price. With compounded drugs selling five times less than Ozempic, things become tricky. From the FDA's perspective, this regulatory tightening could really squeeze Hims' fast-growing revenue stream.

For investors, the warning letter has some reputational and financial implications. Shares dropped about 8% on the news, so there's clearly some concern that there could be more regulatory scrutiny to come. The issue is that, despite obesity and diabetes rates growing, there is only a limited number of customers who can really afford GLP-1 subscriptions. And with the competition so tight between Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk on pricing, they of course don't want a third player undercutting their products in terms of cost. For the exact number of patients Hims has on GLP-1s, we really don't know, but there are some hints.

In a company press release, Hims once said that they “evaluated de-identified data from 13,458 Weight Loss customers prescribed a compounded injectable GLP-1 medication” as part of a study. So we know the number is at least that much, and with Novo's offerings now at $499 for monthly subscriptions, that's almost $7 million in monthly recurring revenue being taken away. In fact, their GLP-1 business generated $190 million in online revenue last quarter.

So while Hims stated its commitment to compliance and also noted that Big Pharma peers like Eli Lilly and Novo also received similar letters, the reality is that Hims' model, mainly due to the price and that heavy direct-to-consumer marketing, will face a higher level of risk under Commissioner Marty Makary's push to stop such promotional practices.

In the near term, we may see Him completely revise its marketing around GLP-1 offerings purely to make the FDA happy. Remember, companies as big as Hims are always in close contact with the FDA, so if we do see a new promotional campaign, it will be a result of new communication with the agency. Longer term, the episode just told us how volatile the stock is to news about regulatory shifts. So while Hims has demonstrated strong growth and profitability, investors should keep in mind the possibility of ongoing oversight from the FDA. But I don't think the development derails the company's broader trajectory to bring new offerings. It only injects some new uncertainty into what had been one of its clearest growth tailwinds.

What about Tariffs?

President Trump announced that starting October 1, any brand-name or patented pharmaceutical imports will be hit with a whopping 100% tariff. However, there's an exception: if the drugmaker is already building a manufacturing plant in the U.S., they will be exempt.

It's another aggressive move from the president, one that he says is meant to pressure Big Pharma to localize production, which should, in theory, lower drug costs in the long run. So, major players like Eli Lilly and Novo have unveiled their multibillion-dollar U.S. plant projects, but the broader pharmaceutical supply chain still remains highly globalized, with many active ingredients sourced overseas.

Drug supply chains and production pipelines are some of the most complicated in the world, with many different parts coming from multiple countries. For Hims, however, the direct impact seems minimal: the company's portfolio is built almost entirely on generic medications, compounded formulations, and telehealth services. These offerings are exempt from the new tariffs. You see, Hims offers compounded semaglutide and tirzepatide formulations through their US partner pharmacies. Compounding means licensed U.S. pharmacies will source the active ingredient and create individualized formulations for patients, which is the main reason they're still allowed to sell compounded GLP-1. By law, compounded products must be made domestically by certified compounding pharmacies. So Hims doesn't actually import finished brand-name drugs and relabel them.

Valaution

Hims is trading at a premium valuation relative to the Seeking Alpha sector peer group, which adds to its risk profile. The market is pricing in some pretty high growth expectations but also raising questions about the sustainability of future growth. On a forward basis, the EV-to-sales ratio sits at 5.26, roughly 49% higher than the sector median of 3.53. Similarly, the forward price-to-sales ratio of 5.30 is elevated; it's 34% above the sector's 3.95 median. It's a premium that signals investors are pricing in continued rapid revenue growth from the new offerings and also margin expansion. To me, the profitability multiples look a bit stretched. The forward EV-to-EBITDA of 38.8 is more than three times the sector median of 12.2, and its forward EV-to-EBIT of 86.5 is nearly 5.6 times higher than the median.

Again, it's screaming that the future earnings improvement is already baked into the stock. We see this again with the forward price-to-earnings of 93.3. It's sharply above the sector's 24.0, despite consensus estimates projecting some meaningful EPS growth through until 2028.

What's Next?

Looking ahead, what I'm watching for Hims will be how management navigates this FDA oversight. I'm expecting them to be in constant communication with one another, and we should see a revised ad campaign follow on GLP-1 marketing. It will also be interesting to see if the subscriber growth can keep pace without any margin erosion. Q3 earnings are expected to come in the first week of November. Their oral testosterone drug seems to be picking up steam, but the next earnings should confirm that quantitatively.

data.title: Economy Growth Revised Upwards
data.description:
The third revision of the latest GDP numbers indicates that the U.S. economy has been growing faster than originally thought.

Consumer spending has been revised upwards, and the result is a growth number that shows the U.S. economy coming in stronger than earlier perceived.

And the numbers seem consistent with an economy being driven by its supply side, an economy that takes its shocks and then returns to its supply-side-driven foundation.

The current approach to monetary policy was introduced by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke in 2009 and has been continued since.

In the past 15 years, there has been one recorded economic recession...the shortest recession in U.S. history.

As can be seen in the chart, the recession took place in the United States in the February-April time period and was quite severe but very short.

Once the swing in the economy took place, the U.S. economy returned to a growth rate that was very similar to the one that had existed before the disturbance.

This is consistent with economies that are being driven by the supply side.

It is highly likely that the growth rate will continue to hang around the 2.5 percent rate in the near future.

However, the future is more uncertain because of the changes President Trump has attempted to impose upon tariff policy and the changes he is hoping to bring to the Federal Reserve policy-making.

It is interesting to me that the compound growth rate of the U.S. economy from the third quarter of 2009 through the second quarter of 2025 has been 2.4 percent.

Policymakers would like the U.S. growth rate to be better than this, but Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve have seemed to be happy with what they have achieved... relatively steady growth, relatively low inflation, and a relatively low rate of unemployment. And they have been happy with only one very short recession during this period of time.

Many monetary commentators would be very happy if the Federal Reserve could continue on a path like this with its stability and with its overall economic results.

Unfortunately, I don't see results like these carrying into the near future.

data.title: Record High Stock Buybacks Could Hint At An AI Bubble
data.description:
Thesis: Companies may be struggling to figure out how to spend their money. Excess cash, especially among big tech companies, and the fear of missing out could fuel an AI bubble as companies make risky and potentially excessive bets on the emerging technology.

I believe Artificial Intelligence will be a civilization-shaping technology and will have a profound impact on the economy and how we organize societies, conduct business, pursue innovation, and more. Back in the 1990s, young though I was, I believed the Internet would reshape society and the economy as well, even as some technology experts suggested that the Internet was more hype than world-changing. Yet while the Internet would prove revolutionary, the dot-com bubble built and popped, and trillions of dollars went up in digital smoke. I believe there’s a risk that an AI bubble is building, not because AI isn’t useful or promising or revolutionary, but instead because high levels of idle cash and limited opportunities to pursue profitable business ventures are fueling potentially excessive and risky investments in AI.

Could Stock Buybacks Hint At An AI Bubble?

Already, high levels of stock buybacks suggest that companies are indeed struggling to spend their money. While I am not an AI skeptic, I must admit that I am a bit of a stock buyback skeptic. Yes, stock buybacks add value to investors, but they don’t grow the company or arguably contribute to the economy as much as expanding production, hiring employees, or conducting research and development. Such activities could directly grow a company’s market cap and drive expansive economic activity. Stock buybacks may make shareholders wealthier, and should they sell their shares, they could invest in economic activities. Yet with asset prices rising, investors may instead simply park their wealth.

Crucially, I believe companies turn to stock buybacks primarily when they simply don’t see profitable opportunities to invest in profitable business opportunities. Companies often don’t want large amounts of cash sitting idle on their balance sheets, and so if there’s no opportunity to put that money to work, then it can make sense to transfer it to shareholders via buybacks. We’ve seen stock buybacks soar to record levels in recent years, and indeed, 2025 is on pace to be a record-breaking year for stock buybacks. This comes even as stock markets have continuously hit all-time highs, with companies seemingly undaunted by the high valuations. This suggests, in my opinion, that there is a lot of cash sitting around idly and some companies simply don’t know what to do with it, except buy back stocks… or… invest in AI.

In total, U.S. companies had announced more than $1 trillion in buybacks as of August, a record-setting pace. Among the biggest spenders on stock buybacks are companies now investing heavily in developing AI. Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) has spent over $20 billion on stock buybacks in 2025 alone, and total buybacks since 2020 are well exceeding $50 billion. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) spent more than $60 billion on buybacks in 2024. Microsoft Corporation has typically (MSFT) been spending $4 to $5 billion per quarter over the last few years.

The constant need to buy back stocks suggests to me that these companies quite simply have so much money on their hands that they don’t know what to do with it, which brings us back to AI. I suspect that the high investments in AI may be at least partially fueled by some large companies simply not knowing what else to do with their money. Meta is apparently set to spend $600 billion on AI over the next three years. Alphabet recently expanded capital expenditures for 2025 from about $75 billion to about $85 billion, with much of the spending focused on AI. (That said, Alphabet also reports that revenue from its cloud-computing unit was up 32% in Q2, fueled by AI services and its in-house chips.) In total, big tech is expected to spend more than $360 billion on AI this year alone and $4 trillion by 2030. Yet revenues remain largely scant. OpenAI is apparently generating about $12 billion in annualized revenue, certainly nothing to sneeze at, but also not much for a company believed to be valued at $500 billion.

These investments are likely being driven by Fear of Missing Out (FOMO) on the next revolutionary technology, but the extremely high amounts of cash that big tech companies have been generating may fuel bad bets and excess. Even amid high AI spending and stock buybacks, many big tech companies have lots of cash and short-term investments on their books.

GOOG Cash and STI

META Cash and STI

MSFT Cash and STI

Increasingly, it seems like old revenue streams, such as ads for Google, are stagnating. Alphabet missed estimates earlier this year amid slow ad growth. Reports indicate that Meta’s Facebook platform is losing users, especially among young demographics, and people are spending less time on the platform. Overall, Microsoft, Google, Meta, and many other big tech companies are continuing to enjoy growing revenues, but there seems to be constant pressure to identify new ways to make money, especially if and when older revenue streams start stagnating. Ultimately, I believe that high levels of cash that risk idling in combination with FOMO could contribute to an AI bubble.

I don’t doubt that AI will prove revolutionary and will ultimately add an immense amount of value to companies. If there is a bubble, which admittedly is speculation, I suspect it will play out like the dot-com bubble. The Magnificent 7 and NASDAQ could take a big hit. Uncompetitive companies, probably smaller startups and mid-cap firms, could be pushed out of the market, and the industry might further consolidate. Still, I suspect that AI as a technology will continue to advance and lead to future innovations, and many companies will not only recover but also go on to enjoy sustained growth. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), among others, took a big hit during the dot-com bubble, but it survived and is today one of the world’s largest companies. AI companies might take a hit during a bubble pop, but that could present an opportunity to buy the dip.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The extent to which AI has been having a direct impact on business and improved productivity right now is quite debatable. Salesforce has apparently cut its customer service staff in nearly half, with agentic AI playing a pivotal role. Yet with generative AI, an MIT study found that 95% of organizations have generated zero returns despite up to $40 billion in investments. Still, we’re in the early stages of the AI disruption, and productivity gains and innovation could quickly accelerate. This could help sustain investments in AI and reduce the risk of a bubble forming and popping. Especially with the biggest companies, like Alphabet and Meta, near-term risks of cash crunches and the like are pretty limited. If shareholders get cold feet and start to sell, say, due to poor earnings owing to high spending on AI, it could create a dip opportunity for long-term investors.

Key Takeaway: I'm Not Panicking, But Will Be Vigilant

Is an AI bubble forming? I still can't say for certain, but I do believe many companies are struggling to identify profitable business ventures. This could encourage some companies, including those pursuing AI, to be rather exuberant with their spending. Add in fears of falling behind competitors, and we could see big tech companies locked in a mad dash to the top. At some point, trimming positions in Mag 7 stocks may be prudent. For now, I believe investors are safe, but if valuations climb, risks may mount.
